(as jun syn Vulsellopsis in Douvillķ 1907) descr. as producing a posterior wing reminiscent of "wanting to escape the envelopment which threatened it by the growth of a sponge." (note spp in his Chalmasia lack this posterior wing). Admittedly, he thinks this is an intermediate between Heligmopsis and Vulsella, and is thus inclined to descr as nestler. In Treatise:N329 as "subequivalve, valves gaping posterodorsally, where margins commonly have jagged or rounded projections ģ posterior edge protruding; surface with concentric lamellae or undulations." No discussion of byssal features so taken to be immobile, unattached, nestling, similar to linguiform Heligmopsis.
recumbent - Schluter et al. 2008, Skelton 2013

arched shell, anteropostero elongate, with slightly undulating posterior gape. Some species noted as being highly variable in form (e.g. N. gaudryi p. 106, but sim. to oyster form?). Taken to be immobile, nestling, but abyssate (cf. its byssate subgenus Exputens).

descr. by Douvillķ 1907:107 and in Treatise N332 as having various shapes, especially in the posterior maringal fissures, and having an irregularly rugose scaly ornamentation. Douville 1907 describes as having a cordiform shell (so inflated sim. to Eligmus). Discusses potential role for the digitations within the posterior gape on p. 108 as [translated] "correspond to the point of penetration of the water current by which the animal fed, while the exit must have taken place in the vicinity of the anal tube, that is to say by the bottom of the sinus: this was thus an anal sinus." Also describes some species as highly variable in form, and implies that there is variable development. Genus taken here to be an immobile, unattached, nestler, same as for Eligmus.

Dhondt 1971:7,33 for Maastrichtian E. membranaceum says "byssal sinus" seems to be absent, and mean umbonal angle is 107║, so above Stanley's 100║ threshold for swimming; E. seymourensis in Zinsmeister & Macellari 1988:267 has umbonal angle of 100║, so right on the edge of swimming, and note a very shallow byssal notch. Their other species E. sadleri with umbonal angle 120║, so swimming, and byssal notch absent.

for type species of genus, E. demissum figured in Treatise:N347 shows umbonal angle of 115║, so above the 100║ threshold for swimming from Stanley. Byssal notch lacking in family, at least in this diagnosis; Maas. species E. splendens from Blank et al. 1974 p. 92 (and figured in a plate) with umbonal angle of 122║ and without a byssal notch; swimming in Ros-Franch et al. 2014

P. aequoris (extant): umbonal angle 90 degrees, very unequal auricles, byssal notch well-developed, but no ctenolium (Dijkstra 1991). P. sigsbeei (extant): umbonal angle 90 degrees, very unequal auricles, byssal notch well-developed, with "Pseudoctenolium" (Waller 1984:212). P. simplicia (Maastrichtian): umbonal angle ~90 degrees, slightly unequal auricles with small byssal sinus (Gardner 1916; used to be Syncylonema).
P. wichmanni (Maastrichtian[?]), confr. in Pectinella acc. to T. Waller comm. to DJ 2017: umbonal angle ~108 degrees, nearly equal auricles, so abyssate.

Dhondt 1971 shows large variance within one Maastrichtian species, but mean is 100║ (N=87 indiv), which is below threshold for swimming in Stanley 1970 Fig. 11. Also mentions deep byssal sinus that reaches half the length of the anterior auricle; CHECK: Hansen & Surlyk 2014

Heligmina descr. by Douvillķ 1907:109-110 has having an anal sinus sim. to Pseudoheligmus. Treatise:N329 desc has having a shell higher than long (more ostreiform), with LV inflated and RV flat. Douvillķ 1907 suggest that being inequivalve marks a shift in the life habit of Heligmina from Eligmus + Pseudoheligmus to being "likely that these molluscs no longer lived in sponges [as he thinks for Eligmus + Pseudoheligmus] but were lying on the bottom of the sea on one of their valves." So genus taken here to be an immobile epifaunal unattached recliner.

Oliver & Drewery 2014:394 "Isorr. species living in seeps are infaunal"; as infaunal in Kiel & Peckman 2019: Table 1, citing Amano & Kiel 2007, although it's not clear from that reference whether a direct functional call, even based on anatomy, was made. Shell lengths for species mostly less than 20mm, with two species up to 46mm and 70mm, so taken to be shallow infaunal given habits for majority of species.

shallow pallial sinus -- Nicol 1953, Johnson et al. 2017; as infaunal in Kiel & Peckmann 2019 Table 1, citing Kiel & Hansen 2015; shell lengths of species from 18mm to 63mm, with most above 50mm, so taken to be semi-infaunal; P. krylovata typically has approx half the shell length above the sediment, forming ōa spatial bridge betw the oxic overlying water & the sulphide-rich sedö (Martin & Goffredi 2012).

Seki & Jenkins 2021:197; Taylor & Glover 2010 for family; shell lengths of species below 20mm, so taken to be shallow infaunal; Amano et al. 2022:297 "Only the smallest species (belonging to the genus Vesicomya) apparently lack symbionts, whereas the larger species host symbionts on which they rely for nutrition."
